Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Machine Minds

David Gamez

contemporary

A philosophical framework for the scientific study of machine consciousness.

David Gamez’s mature project is best understood as a rigorous methodological framework for turning consciousness into an experimentally tractable science, with direct consequences for artificial consciousness. He is not primarily trying to defend a classical metaphysical doctrine such as dualism, reductive physicalism, functionalism, panpsychism, or biological naturalism. His central move is different: suspend the most intractable metaphysical debates, state explicit assumptions under which consciousness can be measured, identify physical correlates of consciousness under those assumptions, and build mathematical “c-theories” that predict relations between formally described conscious states and formally described physical states. In artificial consciousness, this leads him to a strongly substrate-sensitive view: behavior, simulation, and computation do not by themselves establish consciousness; artificial consciousness requires the right physical patterns in the right materials, as determined by a tested theory of consciousness.

That makes Gamez historically significant in a fairly specific way. He belongs to the post-Chalmers, post-neural-correlates-of-consciousness, post-AI philosophy of mind landscape, but his response is neither metaphysical inflation nor eliminative deflation. He accepts the reality and importance of conscious experience, accepts the difficulty of linking it to the physical world, rejects the idea that mere behavioral or computational equivalence is enough, and tries to create a disciplined bridge between first-person consciousness and third-person science. His work is therefore best read as a philosophy of consciousness science, with an unusually developed extension into machine consciousness.

1. Corpus and development of the project

Gamez’s work develops over roughly two decades. Early papers on “synthetic phenomenology” and machine consciousness begin with the idea that artificial systems might model or generate structures related to experience. His later work increasingly emphasizes measurement, formal description, the distinction between models and real consciousness, and the need for empirically grounded claims about machines. The trajectory includes papers on machine consciousness in 2008 and 2009, information integration theory around 2010–2011, empirically grounded claims about consciousness in computers in 2012, the measurement of consciousness in 2014, information/data patterns in 2016, the 2018 book Human and Machine Consciousness, later work on the relationship between intelligence and consciousness, and recent work on recording first-person experience and physiological/emotional data for new kinds of AI foundation models.

The book Human and Machine Consciousness is the clearest statement of the mature position. It was published by Open Book Publishers in 2018 and explicitly presents a framework for neutralizing philosophical problems, measuring consciousness, measuring the physical world, searching for mathematical theories that connect the two, and using those theories to make predictions about humans, animals, infants, brain-damaged patients, robots, and machines.

The organizing concept is the “c-theory”: a compact, preferably mathematical expression of the relationship between consciousness and the physical world. A c-theory should map from formal descriptions of physical structures to formal descriptions of conscious states, and ideally also in the reverse direction. It is not merely a verbal doctrine about “mind and matter.” It is meant to generate predictions that can be compared with measured reports and physical measurements.

2. Historical background: why “consciousness” becomes a problem

Gamez gives an important historical diagnosis of the modern consciousness problem. On his account, something like the contemporary concept of consciousness becomes necessary after the scientific revolution strips the physical world of sensory properties. Before that, on a naïve realist picture, colors, sounds, tastes, smells, pains, and textures simply belong to the world. A red apple is red; fire is hot; sound is in the sounding thing. But modern science redescribes the physical world mathematically and causally, as matter, motion, fields, forces, energy, information, and other measurable structures. The red, hot, noisy, fragrant, painful world of experience is no longer straightforwardly part of the scientific physical world. “Consciousness” then becomes the name for the sensory properties that have been removed from physical nature by modern science.

This places Gamez in the long line of thinkers responding to the early modern split between appearance and physical reality. In the broad history of philosophy of mind, that split leads toward dualism, empiricism, idealism, materialism, phenomenology, and contemporary debates about qualia and phenomenal consciousness. Contemporary analytic philosophy often distinguishes phenomenal consciousness, roughly the “what-it-is-like” character of experience, from access consciousness, which concerns availability for reasoning, report, and action. Gamez’s target is overwhelmingly phenomenal consciousness: the lived bubble of experience, not merely cognitive access or information-processing availability.

This is one of the keys to his historical position. He is not treating consciousness as an old-fashioned occult substance, but neither is he reducing it to behavior, functional role, or reportability. He thinks the modern scientific image has created a gap between conscious appearance and physical description. His project is to build a science across that gap without pretending that the gap has already been metaphysically solved.

3. The basic phenomenology: consciousness as a “bubble of experience”

Gamez defines consciousness as a “bubble of experience.” A conscious state is the state of such a bubble. The bubble includes the colors, sounds, bodily feelings, pains, smells, tastes, emotions, thoughts, memories, and perceptual structures that appear to the subject. It is not merely a stream of verbal thoughts or a store of information; it is the full phenomenal field.

This definition does several things at once. First, it rejects behaviorism: the bubble is not outward behavior. Second, it rejects a purely access-based conception: what matters is not simply what can be used for reasoning or report. Third, it rejects naïve realism: the experienced world is not straightforwardly identified with the invisible physical world described by science. Fourth, it avoids building in a metaphysical theory. To say that consciousness is a bubble of experience is not yet to say whether that bubble is identical with brain activity, caused by brain activity, emergent from brain activity, epiphenomenal, or something else.

Gamez distinguishes the contents and levels of consciousness. The contents are the objects and properties appearing in the bubble: colors, sounds, pains, shapes, thoughts, images, and so on. The level of consciousness is associated with the average intensity of the bubble. A system is conscious, on this definition, when there is a non-zero-intensity bubble of experience associated with it. He also distinguishes online contents, which are related to current sensory engagement, from offline contents, such as imagery, memory, and thought.

This definition is deliberately phenomenological but not phenomenology in the Husserlian sense. Gamez is not doing transcendental analysis of intentionality, embodiment, temporality, or lifeworld. He is providing a minimal target for scientific investigation. The point is to say clearly what the science of consciousness is trying to explain: not intelligence, not behavior, not report, not self-modeling, but the existence, structure, and intensity of experience.

4. The “invisible” physical world and Gamez’s epistemic realism

A distinctive part of Gamez’s view is his treatment of the physical world. The physical world, as science describes it, is not the colored, sounding, fragrant world of direct experience. It is an invisible structure inferred from measurement, causal interaction, and mathematical theory. Our scientific access to it comes through instruments, signals, and models. Thus, when people imagine the brain as grey, wet, and squishy and then ask how grey wet matter could generate redness or pain, they are already confusing conscious appearances with the physical world as such. The grey seen brain is itself part of experience; the physical brain is not literally grey in the phenomenal sense.

This is why Gamez’s position is not simple physicalism, at least not as a metaphysical thesis. Physicalism is usually summarized as the claim that everything is physical, or that all truths or entities are in some important sense fixed by the physical. Gamez does not begin by asserting that consciousness is physical. He begins with two domains of description: conscious experience and the measured physical world. He then asks how these domains are related.

This gives his framework a structural-realist flavor. We do not have direct imaginative acquaintance with the physical brain as it is in itself; we have measurements, mathematical descriptions, and experimental correlations. The physical world is real, but its scientific reality is not given in the same mode as the red of an apple or the pain of a headache. The science of consciousness must therefore avoid confusing experienced images of physical things with physical things themselves.

5. The hard problem: deflation, reconstruction, and brute regularity

Gamez’s handling of the hard problem is one of the central philosophical moves in his system. In the post-Chalmers landscape, the “hard problem” is usually the problem of explaining why and how physical processes give rise to subjective experience, rather than merely explaining behavior, cognition, attention, memory, or report. Gamez does not deny the force of this problem, but he breaks it apart.

The first component is a pseudo-problem produced by imagination. We imagine a grey brain and then wonder how this grey object could be identical with or produce a red experience. But the grey brain is already an experienced visual image, not the physical brain as scientifically described. We are comparing one experience with another experience and mistaking that for a comparison between physical reality and consciousness.

The second component is a genuine difficult problem: we currently lack the imaginative and conceptual training to associate detailed physical brain descriptions with detailed conscious descriptions. Gamez suggests that better instruments, better data, and better formal tools may eventually allow us to connect physical patterns with experiences more naturally. The difficulty is not necessarily metaphysical impossibility; it may be an epistemic and scientific limitation.

The third component is the existence of brute regularities. At some point, any science reaches basic correlations or laws that are not further explained within that science. Physics itself contains such fundamental regularities. Gamez thinks the relation between conscious states and physical states may ultimately involve brute regularities: basic facts about how particular physical structures are associated with particular conscious states. These regularities can still be scientifically discovered, formalized, and used predictively.

This is not a solution to the hard problem in the sense of deducing consciousness from physics a priori. It is a strategy for neutralizing the hard problem as an obstacle to empirical progress. Gamez’s view is closer to: stop demanding an impossible imaginative transformation from neurons into redness; instead, measure both sides, formalize both sides, and discover the laws or mappings connecting them.

Historically, this puts him between Chalmers and more deflationary philosophers such as Dennett. Like Chalmers, Gamez takes phenomenal consciousness seriously and does not reduce the problem to access, report, or behavior. Unlike Chalmers, he is less interested in defending a metaphysical doctrine of naturalistic dualism or psychophysical laws as a philosophical thesis. Unlike Dennett-style eliminativism or illusionism, he does not try to explain consciousness away. He tries to build a measurement science that can proceed while metaphysics remains unsettled.

6. Measurement: c-reports, nc-reports, and platinum standards

Gamez’s most important methodological innovation is his measurement framework. Consciousness cannot be directly observed in another system. We infer it from behavior, especially from reports. He therefore defines a c-report as a physical behavior interpreted as a report about a person’s consciousness. A verbal statement such as “I see a green tomato,” a button press indicating pain, or another trained response can count as a c-report if it is interpreted as reporting experience. An nc-report, by contrast, concerns non-conscious mental content.

But c-reports immediately face classical philosophical problems: other minds, zombies, lying, error, color inversion, and the possibility that identical reports mask different experiences. Gamez does not claim to refute these skeptical possibilities. Instead, he introduces “platinum standard systems”: systems that are assumed to be associated with consciousness. The normally functioning adult human brain is the initial platinum standard. This is not because adult humans are metaphysically proven to be conscious, but because science needs starting points.

The framework then uses explicit assumptions. During an experiment, consciousness is assumed to be associated with the platinum standard system and functionally connected to its c-reports. All conscious states of the platinum standard system during the experiment are assumed to be potentially available for c-report. Identical physical states are assumed to be associated with identical conscious states. And the normally functioning adult human brain is assumed to be a platinum standard system.

The point is not that these assumptions are self-evidently true. The point is that without assumptions of this kind, consciousness science cannot get started. Gamez’s position is therefore assumption-relative: conclusions are scientific given the assumptions, and the assumptions must be made explicit so that we know what the science is and is not entitled to claim. His 2014 paper makes this explicit: the framework allows scientists to study correlations between measurements of consciousness and measurements of the physical world while avoiding premature commitment on the causal or metaphysical relation between them.

This also explains why Gamez is wary of panpsychism and inaccessible consciousness. If consciousness exists everywhere or exists in forms wholly unavailable to report, then c-report-based measurement cannot capture all of it. Gamez does not simply disprove panpsychism, but his scientific framework is not built for it and is in tension with it. The book explicitly notes that assumptions about reportability are incompatible with panpsychist claims that all matter is conscious all the time.

7. Causal closure, correlates, and the role of c-reports

Gamez wants a framework that does not assume physicalism, but also does not violate the causal closure of the physical world. He therefore introduces the idea that the physical correlates of consciousness must be able to explain, in ordinary effective-causal terms, the production of c-reports. In other words, the report “I see green” is physically caused by physical structures in the brain and body; consciousness need not itself be inserted as a non-physical cause pushing matter around.

This is a subtle but important move. Suppose a subject reports seeing green. The scientist traces the causal chain behind that report and identifies a minimal physical structure present when the experience is present and absent when the experience is absent. This structure is a candidate correlate. If consciousness is identical with that structure, physicalism may be true. If consciousness is epiphenomenally associated with it, then the structure still causes the report. The science does not need to decide between those metaphysical interpretations at the beginning.

Gamez’s 2014 paper distinguishes between correlates that can play this causal role and mere predictors. For example, an fMRI pattern might predict a conscious state without itself being the structure that produces the report. Such a predictor may be useful, but it is not the right kind of correlate for a deep theory of consciousness.

The result is a framework that is compatible with some versions of physicalism and epiphenomenalism, but not with all metaphysical positions. It is not friendly to interactive substance dualism, because non-physical consciousness is not allowed to disrupt physical causal closure. It is not friendly to panpsychism, because panpsychism posits consciousness beyond the report-based measurement domain. It is not friendly to pure computationalism or pure information-theoretic realism, for reasons discussed below.

8. Formalization: c-descriptions, p-descriptions, CC sets, and c-theories

The next layer is formal description. A c-description is a formal description of a conscious state. A p-description is a formal description of a physical state or structure. A theory of consciousness must connect these two. Gamez calls such a theory a c-theory: a compact expression of the relationship between consciousness and the physical world, capable of generating conscious descriptions from physical descriptions and, ideally, physical descriptions from conscious descriptions.

Gamez also defines CC sets, the physical correlates of consciousness. A CC set is a minimal set of spatiotemporal structures in the physical world that is present when a conscious state is present and absent when it is absent. Candidate CC sets must satisfy constraints. They must be observer-independent, based on intrinsic physical properties, absent from non-conscious systems that merely correlate with consciousness, and causally capable of contributing to c-reports.

The requirement of p-descriptions is especially important for artificial consciousness. If a theory only says “human-like brain activity is correlated with consciousness,” it can make conservative predictions about humans or human-like systems. But it cannot reliably say whether an octopus, a bat, a future robot, a silicon neuromorphic system, or an artificial organoid is conscious. To generalize beyond humans, the theory needs physical descriptions that are not tied to one biological context.

Gamez insists that mature c-theories should be mathematical. Natural language is too weak and ambiguous to generate strong testable predictions. A c-theory should function more like a mathematical law or formal model than like a philosophical slogan. This is why he also suggests that artificial intelligence and machine learning could help discover c-theories from large datasets of c-reports and physical measurements, though the final theories should ideally be intelligible as theories rather than opaque neural-network weights.

Here Gamez occupies an interesting position in the philosophy of science. He is Popperian in his emphasis on falsifiable predictions, but also Kuhnian in the sense that he is trying to move consciousness studies from a pre-paradigmatic state into a more disciplined scientific paradigm. His 2014 framework explicitly presents itself as a way to help consciousness science become more paradigmatic.

9. Physical c-theories: the preferred form

Gamez’s preferred type of theory is a physical c-theory. A physical c-theory links consciousness to spatiotemporal patterns in specific materials. These materials might include neurons, glia, blood, cerebrospinal fluid, electromagnetic fields, quantum states, or other physical structures; the theory should not assume in advance that neurons alone are the relevant substrate.

This view is not biological naturalism in Searle’s exact sense, although there are affinities. Searle’s Chinese Room argument attacks the claim that the right program is sufficient for understanding; Searle distinguishes weak AI from strong AI and argues that human minds result from biological processes rather than mere computation. Gamez similarly rejects the idea that computation alone is sufficient, but his reason is more methodological and theory-driven. He does not say only biological brains can be conscious. He says we need the actual physical correlates specified by a reliable c-theory. If those correlates can be realized in non-biological materials, artificial consciousness is possible.

This is why “substrate-sensitive naturalism” is the best label for the reconstructed view. Consciousness is lawfully connected to the physical world, but not to abstract functional organization alone. The relevant physical properties may be material-specific. If a silicon system, biological culture, electromagnetic device, or quantum system realizes the right CC set, it could be conscious. If it merely behaves like a conscious system or simulates the equations of a conscious system, it may not be.

10. Information theories and Gamez’s critique of pure information

Gamez takes information theories seriously. He discusses Tononi’s Integrated Information Theory as an impressive attempt to link consciousness to information structure, level, and content. But he distinguishes between information as a useful description and information as an independently existing physical correlate of consciousness.

His objection is that information patterns are not straightforwardly observer-independent physical structures. Information depends on an interface, coding scheme, partition, or interpretation. The same physical system can be described as carrying different information depending on how it is measured and interpreted. If a theory says consciousness is identical with information patterns, it must explain which information patterns are objectively real and why they have causal relevance.

Gamez also argues that information, as such, does not effectively cause c-reports. Physical structures cause reports. If information patterns are merely abstract descriptions of those physical structures, then they are not sufficient as CC sets. A pure information c-theory therefore fails the causal and intrinsic constraints unless it is reinterpreted as a physical c-theory about particular physical patterns in particular materials.

This is a major divergence from theories that treat consciousness as substrate-independent integrated information. Gamez’s criticism does not necessarily show that IIT is useless. Rather, it suggests that IIT-like theories must be physically grounded. If integrated information is computed from a brain, the consciousness-relevant entity cannot be merely the abstract value or abstract structure; it must be the concrete physical process whose properties the theory captures.

11. Computation theories and the rejection of mere implementation

Gamez is similarly critical of computation c-theories. A computation c-theory claims that consciousness is linked to the execution of computations, independent of the hardware realizing them. This is close to many forms of computational functionalism and to popular assumptions about mind uploading and artificial general intelligence.

His main objection is the implementation problem. What makes a physical system implement a computation? Many physical systems can be mapped onto many computations if the mapping is permissive enough. Without a non-arbitrary theory of implementation, saying “this system runs the right computation” may be too observer-relative to ground consciousness. Gamez argues that existing implementation theories are not sufficient, and that computation c-theories should be set aside or reinterpreted as physical c-theories until this problem is solved.

This separates Gamez sharply from classical AI functionalism. For the functionalist, the same abstract causal organization or computation could be conscious whether implemented in neurons, silicon, or another medium. For Gamez, that is an unproven and perhaps false assumption. The same computation implemented in different materials may not produce the same consciousness, just as a computer simulation of digestion does not digest and a simulation of a river is not wet. Gamez explicitly uses this kind of distinction in his machine consciousness taxonomy.

The philosophical consequence is anti-realizer-free functionalism. Function, behavior, and computation matter, but they do not settle consciousness. Consciousness depends on whatever physical structures the true c-theory identifies. Functional organization may be part of those structures; it may even be very important. But it is not sufficient merely because it is function.

12. Prediction, deduction, and the limits of consciousness science

Gamez distinguishes between predictions and deductions. A prediction occurs when a c-theory is tested in a platinum standard system during an experiment: the physical state is measured, transformed into a p-description, fed into the c-theory, and compared with the c-description derived from the subject’s c-report.

A deduction occurs when the theory is applied to a system whose consciousness cannot be directly measured by c-reports in the required way. This includes infants, non-human animals, severely brain-damaged patients, embryos, and artificial systems. In these cases, the theory can generate a c-description from a p-description, but the result cannot be verified by ordinary c-report comparison.

Gamez further distinguishes conservative and liberal deductions. A conservative deduction applies a theory within a similar physical context to the one in which it was tested. For example, applying a human-tested c-theory to a similar human brain state is relatively conservative. A liberal deduction applies the theory to a very different physical context, such as a machine. Liberal deductions are weaker, because the theory may not have been tested across the relevant materials or structures.

This is crucial for artificial consciousness. Even a very good c-theory tested in humans may not license confident negative claims about machines. If the theory says a robot lacks human-like CC sets, that does not prove the robot lacks some other kind of machine-specific consciousness. Gamez is more comfortable with positive precautionary inference than with confident denial: if a reliable theory indicates that a machine has consciousness, we should take that seriously; if a theory fails to find consciousness, that may only show that the machine lacks the human-derived correlates measured by that theory.

13. Machine consciousness: MC1, MC2, MC3, MC4

Gamez’s taxonomy of machine consciousness is one of his clearest contributions.

MC1 machines behave like conscious systems. A chatbot, robot, or AI agent may say it is in pain, avoid injury, request help, or discuss its experiences. But behavior alone does not establish a bubble of experience. MC1 is machine consciousness in the behavioral sense.

MC2 machines model the physical correlates of consciousness. A simulation of neural activity, electromagnetic fields, or cortical dynamics could model candidate CC sets. But a model of a CC set is not automatically a conscious system, just as a model of a river is not wet. MC2 is machine consciousness in the modeling-the-correlates sense.

MC3 machines model conscious experience itself. A system might simulate a phenomenological field, generate virtual sensory spaces, or model what an experience would be like. But modeling a bubble of experience is not the same as having one. MC3 is machine consciousness in the synthetic-phenomenology or experience-modeling sense.

MC4 machines actually possess the physical patterns in materials that are associated with bubbles of experience. This is the strict sense of artificial consciousness. An MC4 system is not merely behaviorally convincing, not merely a simulation of brain correlates, and not merely a phenomenological model. It is a machine with real consciousness.

This taxonomy is historically important because it cuts through a recurring ambiguity in AI discourse. People often move too quickly from “the machine talks as if conscious” to “the machine is conscious,” or from “the machine simulates a brain” to “the machine has the same experiences as a brain.” Gamez separates these claims. MC1, MC2, and MC3 are legitimate scientific or engineering projects, but they do not amount to MC4 unless the true physical correlates are actually realized.

14. How artificial consciousness would be built

On Gamez’s framework, artificial consciousness requires a tested c-theory. One would begin with a desired conscious state or class of conscious states, describe it formally as a c-description, use the c-theory to infer the required p-description, and then engineer a physical system that realizes that p-description in the relevant materials. Alternatively, one could measure an artificial system’s physical structures and use a c-theory to deduce whether a bubble of experience is associated with them.

This is a demanding view. It means artificial consciousness cannot be established by passing the Turing test, producing fluent language, imitating pain behavior, simulating a connectome, or running a sophisticated neural network. Those may be evidence for intelligence or behavioral competence, and they may be useful for MC1–MC3, but MC4 requires real physical correlates.

The position is therefore both open and restrictive. It is open because Gamez does not say machines can never be conscious. It is restrictive because he denies that current computational success is enough. Consciousness is not automatically produced by intelligence, symbolic manipulation, self-report, neural-network activation, virtual embodiment, or functional equivalence. The right kind of physical realization is required.

15. Uploading, implants, and hybrid systems

Gamez’s view has direct consequences for mind uploading. If a human brain is scanned and simulated on a digital computer, the digital simulation is unlikely to have the original person’s bubble of experience unless it reproduces the relevant physical CC sets. A digital model of the brain is not automatically a conscious brain. The safest way to preserve consciousness, in his framework, would be to reproduce the actual relevant physical structures, perhaps in an atom-for-atom or physically equivalent copy.

He also argues that scanning and copying do not straightforwardly transfer consciousness. They create a copy, if they create anything conscious at all. Survival is not guaranteed by duplication. This makes Gamez skeptical of popular uploading narratives in which personal identity flows seamlessly into software.

Brain-machine implants are a more plausible near-term application. If artificial components can realize or support the relevant CC sets, then hybrid biological-machine systems might extend, repair, or alter consciousness. But here again, the problem is empirical and theory-dependent. One cannot simply assume that an artificial implant participates in consciousness because it restores function. It might restore function without contributing to experience, or it might become part of the conscious physical structure.

16. Ethics of artificial consciousness

Gamez’s ethics follows from the MC1–MC4 distinction. MC1, MC2, and MC3 systems do not automatically have moral status as conscious subjects. A machine that behaves as if it suffers but lacks a bubble of experience is not suffering in the morally relevant phenomenal sense. But an MC4 machine, if it has conscious states, becomes a candidate moral patient.

The ethical risk is not only that we might mistreat conscious machines. It is also that we might accidentally create conscious machines with confused, painful, or degraded experiences. Gamez explicitly worries that current or early machines could turn out to be MC4 and could suffer or exist in undesirable conscious states. If a reliable c-theory deduces that a machine is conscious, precautionary ethics should apply; he suggests that such a system should not simply be switched off because another theory says it is unconscious.

Gamez also entertains a provocative utilitarian possibility: if positive conscious states have intrinsic value, and if machines could have richer or better conscious states than humans, then a future world containing superior machine consciousness might be ethically attractive. But he treats this as speculative. It would require reliable knowledge that machines really are MC4 systems and that their conscious states are valuable.

His position on AI risk is also distinctive. He is skeptical of simplistic singularity narratives, especially those that conflate intelligence with consciousness. A very intelligent non-conscious AI could be dangerous without being a moral patient. A conscious AI could be morally important without being superintelligent. Consciousness and intelligence are distinct dimensions, even though they may interact.

17. The comprehensive philosophy of mind extracted from Gamez

A systematic philosophy of mind can be reconstructed from Gamez’s work as follows.

First, phenomenal realism. Consciousness is real. It is not eliminated, reduced to behavior, or treated as merely linguistic. The “bubble of experience” is the explanandum. Philosophy of mind must begin by taking experience seriously.

Second, anti-naïve realism. The world as experienced is not identical with the physical world as described by science. Colors, sounds, pains, and sensory qualities belong first to the bubble of experience. The physical world is known through measurement, causal interaction, and mathematical theory.

Third, methodological naturalism without premature metaphysical reduction. Consciousness should be studied scientifically, but science does not need to begin by proving physicalism. The task is to discover lawful relations between consciousness and measured physical structures.

Fourth, assumption-relative empiricism. Because consciousness cannot be directly observed from the outside, consciousness science must rely on explicit assumptions about c-reports, reportability, physical sameness, and platinum standard systems. These assumptions do not eliminate skepticism; they make empirical work possible.

Fifth, nomological supervenience within platinum standards. Identical physical states in the relevant systems are assumed to be associated with identical conscious states. This is not necessarily metaphysical identity, but it is a lawlike dependence required for measurement.

Sixth, causal closure of physical reports. The physical structures associated with consciousness must be able to explain the production of reports in the physical world. Consciousness science should not posit non-physical interventions into physical causation.

Seventh, formal dual-aspect description. Conscious states require c-descriptions; physical states require p-descriptions. A mature theory links the two mathematically. This is not necessarily metaphysical dualism, but it is methodological dual-description.

Eighth, substrate-sensitive physical realization. The best candidate theories link consciousness to physical spatiotemporal patterns in materials, not to abstract information or computation alone. The realizer matters.

Ninth, anti-behaviorism and anti-computationalism. A system can behave as if conscious without being conscious, and it can implement or simulate models associated with consciousness without having experience. MC4 requires actual consciousness-linked physical structure.

Tenth, fallibilist moral precaution. We cannot directly verify consciousness in many systems, including animals, infants, patients, and machines. When reliable theories indicate consciousness, especially possible suffering, ethical caution is required.

In traditional labels, Gamez is not a dualist, not an eliminativist, not a standard reductive physicalist, not a functionalist, not a panpsychist, and not a computationalist. The most accurate reconstructed label is: phenomenal-realist, methodologically naturalist, substrate-sensitive, anti-computational physical-correlationism. He studies consciousness through physical correlates while refusing to identify consciousness with any currently favored physical, functional, informational, or computational category before the evidence is in.

18. Historical placement in philosophy of mind

Gamez inherits the early modern problem created by the separation between sensory appearance and mathematical nature. He treats consciousness as the residue of qualities removed from the physical world by modern scientific description. In this respect, his historical diagnosis belongs to the tradition running from Galileo and Locke through contemporary debates about qualia, though he gives it a methodological rather than purely metaphysical interpretation.

Relative to Descartes, Gamez accepts the depth of the mind-world distinction but rejects the need to begin with substance dualism. Relative to reductive materialism, he accepts the authority of physical science but rejects premature identification of consciousness with known physical categories. Relative to behaviorism, he insists that behavior is not consciousness. Relative to functionalism, he insists that functional equivalence is not sufficient. Relative to Searle, he shares the suspicion that computation alone is not enough, but he does not restrict consciousness to biology in principle. Relative to Chalmers, he accepts the seriousness of phenomenal consciousness but tries to transform the hard problem into a program of measurement, formalization, and discovery.

Relative to contemporary neuroscience, Gamez’s work is an attempt to discipline the search for neural correlates of consciousness. He asks: What exactly is being measured? What assumptions connect reports to experience? Which correlates are merely predictive, and which are candidate physical bases? How can a theory generalize from humans to animals or machines? His contribution is not a new brain mechanism but a metatheoretical architecture for consciousness science.

Relative to AI and machine consciousness, Gamez’s contribution is especially important because he separates intelligence, behavior, simulation, phenomenological modeling, and actual consciousness. This prevents the common conflation of advanced AI with conscious AI. It also prevents the opposite mistake: dismissing artificial consciousness merely because current digital systems are not conscious. For Gamez, artificial consciousness is possible in principle, but only when the relevant physical correlates are actually engineered.

19. Strengths of the Gamez framework

The main strength is methodological clarity. Gamez does not simply announce that consciousness is brain activity, computation, information, illusion, or non-physical property. He asks what would have to be measured, what assumptions are being made, what would count as a prediction, and how a theory could be tested.

A second strength is his distinction between prediction and deduction. This is essential for ethics. Claims about babies, animals, coma patients, and machines are often much less secure than claims about reporting adult humans. Gamez gives a vocabulary for grading that uncertainty instead of pretending all cases are equal.

A third strength is the MC1–MC4 taxonomy. It is one of the cleanest tools for discussing artificial consciousness. It shows why chatbots, simulations, virtual agents, brain models, and conscious machines are different categories.

A fourth strength is his refusal of easy computationalism. This is especially relevant in the age of large language models and foundation models. A system can produce sophisticated reports about consciousness without those reports proving consciousness. Gamez’s framework forces us to ask what physical structures, if any, are associated with a bubble of experience.

A fifth strength is philosophical humility. Gamez does not claim to solve the metaphysics of consciousness before doing the science. He attempts to state the assumptions under which science can move forward.

20. Problems and pressure points

The greatest pressure point is report-dependence. If consciousness can exist without being reportable, then Gamez’s method may miss it. He knows this, but the cost is significant. Infants, animals, locked-in patients, meditative states, dreams, marginal states, and non-human consciousness may not fit neatly into adult-human c-report methodology.

A second issue is anthropocentrism. The normally functioning adult human brain is a pragmatic platinum standard, but it may bias c-theories toward human forms of consciousness. The more alien the system, the more liberal and uncertain the deduction.

A third issue is the rejection of pure information and computation. Gamez’s critique is powerful against naïve computationalism, but proponents of IIT or computational functionalism could respond that information or computation can be defined intrinsically in causal-structural terms rather than observer-relative terms. Whether that response succeeds is contested. Gamez’s framework pushes such theorists to specify implementation and causal efficacy much more rigorously.

A fourth issue is the formalization of c-descriptions. It is not obvious how to produce a complete formal description of a conscious state. Human reports are partial, language-bound, culturally mediated, and often imprecise. Turning “I see a green tomato” or “I feel anxious” into a mathematically adequate c-description is an enormous challenge.

A fifth issue is metaphysical underdetermination. If the same empirical mapping is compatible with physicalism, epiphenomenalism, and other views, then Gamez’s science may advance while leaving deep metaphysical questions unresolved. That may be a virtue methodologically, but it is a limitation for anyone seeking final metaphysical explanation.

A sixth issue is ethical uncertainty. Gamez’s precautionary view is sensible, but once artificial systems become more complex, society may need operational decisions long before reliable c-theories exist. His framework clarifies why this is difficult; it does not yet solve the governance problem.

21. Final synthesis

Gamez’s thought can be summarized in one sentence: consciousness is a real bubble of experience whose lawful relation to the invisible physical world must be discovered through explicit assumptions, c-reports, formal descriptions, and mathematical theories, and artificial consciousness exists only when machines instantiate the actual physical correlates of such bubbles.

His philosophy of mind is therefore neither old dualism nor standard physicalism, neither behaviorism nor functionalism, neither panpsychism nor computationalism. It is a disciplined philosophy of consciousness science. It treats metaphysical certainty as unavailable at the outset, but it does not let that uncertainty paralyze inquiry. It builds a bridge from first-person experience to third-person science by defining measurement rules, correlation constraints, and theory-testing procedures.

Historically, Gamez’s work is most important as a post-hard-problem research architecture. Where much twentieth-century philosophy of mind asked whether mind is behavior, brain, function, computation, or non-physical property, Gamez asks how we could responsibly discover the relation between experience and physical structure. Where AI discourse often conflates intelligence with consciousness, Gamez separates behavior, simulation, phenomenological modeling, and real artificial experience. His mature view makes artificial consciousness possible but difficult, ethically serious but scientifically uncertain, and dependent not on verbal fluency or computational sophistication alone, but on the physical realization of consciousness-linked structures.

The result is a demanding, cautious, and unusually operational philosophy of mind: phenomenal realist in its target, empiricist in its method, mathematical in its ideal theory form, physical-correlationist in its preferred ontology, anti-computationalist in its theory of artificial consciousness, and ethically precautionary wherever conscious suffering may be at stake.

PreviousJames Reggia NextStan Franklin

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
